title: 2025/08/13 - SSC
displayTitle: 2025-08-13 - Social Services and Community Committee - Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Social Workers Registration Board
topic: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Social Workers Registration Board
description: Wednesday, 13 August 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Social Workers Registration Board Hearing of Evidence Social Workers Registration Board - Mike Munnelly, Chair - Sarah Clark, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Social Workers Registration Board. Social Workers Registration Board. Mike Munnelly, Chair

chunks.text: Good morning and welcome to the Social Services and Community Committee. Uh, we have you, uh, until nine 40, um, or 9 35. Um, but if we wanted to introduce yourselves and, uh, give us a bit of an overview and, um, I, questions from the committee. Appreciate it. Kia took, uh, um, and thank you for the warm welcome.

My name's Mike Ali and I am the chair of the Social Work Registration Board, but my relatively new chair appointed in January of this year. Um, to my right, Sarah Clarke is the chief executive who I think you're aware of. And behind us, we've got a strong cohort of support of some of the other leaders in SWR.

Being able to answer some of, some of your questions, we struggle a bit. Um, so we provided an, I know you've got, uh, our answers to your written questions and we recently provided a PowerPoint presentation about who we are and what we do. Um, I'm not proposing that we run you through that 'cause actually keen to have that conversation. Um, but I do want to kind of make some opening and scene setting remarks, really.

Um, so while the SWRB has been around for over 20 years, mandatory registration of social workers only came into effect in 2021. And that result in a quite significant period of growth, both in terms of the numbers of social workers on the register. So between 2019 and 2022, the number of social workers on practicing certificates doubled.

And, and we're now, I think at about 12,000 social workers on the register. That growth and meeting that demand meant there was a growth in the size of the organization. It brought challenges. Um, financial ones, uh, were one of the first, the bulk of our income is fee-based. We rely on the fees and just the nature of that business means there is inevitable lag.

Um, so, um, so the challenge of finances, the challenge then in just building systems to manage the new statutory responsibilities. And, um, and we've seen an increase in complaints. Um, and that's something that's continued through to the present day. Um, and essentially having gone through that startup phase, I think we are really now at the point where we've got to relatively steady state.

Um, and it really is time to take the next step and continue our growth and mature as a regulator. Um, so in terms of that, just a few highlights. So what we're, um, so we're progressing the amendments that, the changes that came with the amendments to the act for which we are very grateful, which will improve our regulatory practice and processes.

We're moving to implement a new regulatory strategy which will provide greater transparency both for social workers, employers, and importantly the public. We recently agreed some new education standards for social work training, uh, which will, is responding to the workforce needs. We know we have. And that's very much focused and geared towards both incentivizing excellence in teaching and some flexibility in delivery.

We continues to grow our really rich source of data. We've probably got an amazing data set about the workforce. Um, and, uh, we have an incredibly, uh, high response rate from social 'cause when we go out and seek their views. Um, and that, uh, data has been very instrumental in what will, in establishing what will be the first national strategy and action plan for the social worker cross.

And importantly, we have a continued, um, focus on prudent fin financial management, um, and what's meant that this year have been conscious of the environment we've been able to hold the fees for social workers and for the board. Um, that affordability question is very live and it will continue to be a, a feature in our minds.

Um, having kind of given that, um, highlight, I guess the other bit I kind of want to just say is go back to the beginning. So I am the new chair, um, and I've also been joined by three other new members. And we are current recruiting for a further one. So we're a board of seven. Um, so that's five changes. That's quite a significant change.

And I'm saying that 'cause I really want to acknowledge the previous board and chair 'cause they lay laid the foundation for us to progress some of the things that we want to do and see how it's important and, and ending on a personal. So I am a registered social worker. I've been a social worker since 1978, um, practice both here and in the uk.

Um, I am proud of my profession. Um, uh, and, and one of the reasons I'm on the body because our, I'm committed to the importance and their value of registration. Social work can, has the potential to do tremendous good. It also has the potential to cause harm. And that's why regulation becomes important.

So as a regulator, as registration, it not only enhances the profession, it can provide build trust and competence in the public in what we do. And importantly, the people we serve have an absolute right that when a social worker knocks on their door, they know what they're doing, they know how to do it, they do it well, and they know when to seek help.

And that's really important if we're gonna really achieve the potential that social work has to make change. I'll now hand you over to Sarah. Nice morning. Um, um, I'm not really going to add much more because I, I think the most rich part will probably be in the questions from, from the committee.

Uh, the only thing I wanted to add, um, 'cause some of these members we, we, uh, met earlier in the year with the amendment bill. I just wanted to express my thanks really, um, for, for supporting those changes. But also to let you know just how the debate and, and the comments from MPS in the house on that legislation.

It means so much to the profession because they are forever on the receiving end of criticism. To have the contribution and value of the profession acknowledged in that way is really significant. And as we strive for that enhanced professionalism, um, those are the things that matter.

And, and the reason I think it's important, I mean, I'm not, I'm not a registered social worker. Um, but I believe in the profession and, and, and what it can do. But it's really important that for the participants and those who use social work services, who are often in an involuntary situation, they don't choose necessarily to engage with a social worker.

That we find ways to build trust in the system. Because, because that's the only way it's gonna work. And that's the only way we're gonna generate change if, if people actually believe the system can work. So we see ourselves as an important contributor, not the sole one, but at a system level, we've got our part to play.

Um, and that's really the ethos behind the, the approach we take. So I, I thought perhaps you might have specific questions about the presentation or, um, or just in general. It's, it's probably, uh, it would be maybe 2022 the last time we came here for this more general conversation.

Um, we've talked about bits of legislation previously, so it is really like, would you like a bit of history? Would you like a sense of where we are going? Um, you know, I've been with the board for eight years now, so I came in just as the legislation was coming in to be changed. So I've been on that journey. So if you'd like to know, how did we get here?

Um, the highlights, the lowlights or where, where do we wanna get to? Um, um, I can draw on that and, and help chart you where we are headed. Well, uh, thank you very much both of that and, uh, just, just like saying what, thank you very much for all the work that you do and also the, the work that the, um, social workers across New Zealand do that's hugely important to our communities and individuals and families, um, all, all across the country.

And just on that, I might just be checking, ask the very first question, um, just, I just, how do you, um, uh, manage that relationship across, you know, from the bottom of the south, the top of the north, um, with a relatively small board and this, uh, professionalization process that you've been going through. Mm-hmm. So maybe, shall I? Mm-hmm. Yeah.

So, so, um, so well, you know, we know that social workers are spread from the bottom to the top. Um, and, and probably it's where some of that post COVID environment has given us some new tools with a degree of acceptance that, you know, the online, um, hooey so we can reach out without necessarily always having to have the cost of actually traveling to being places in person.

Um, and, and that that applies to not just the social workers or the applicants, it also applies to employers. And I think the role of employers is we, we are going to be spending a bit more time in the future because employers are pivotal in creating the sort of environment in which social workers can operate safely. Um, and we are in the process of refreshing our strategic communications and engagement plan.

So thinking about audience's, location, that sort of segmentation, um, is really a live issue for us at the moment because we know that we will only make a difference when people can see and hear us. And we've, we spent, you know, time building stuff and now we need to be looking out more and building, I guess there's that concept of the social license because our work only works when the profession understands and has buy-in.

So that's sort of, we've got the now approach, but we're working on refining for the future as well. Just one, one last question from me on this general point is just the how, well the digital tools that support that, um, I guess the underlying infrastructure and then that out was engagement. How, how will those work with You? I, I would almost think from that question that you are on our board, um, um, it's a very, so the board, the board board, um, r and d challenging us in this space.

We have a, um, we have a program of enhancement with our main database, which is imis, uh, and which itself is A CRM, but we don't use it that way. It's really at the heart of us having the high quality data and insights that, that we want. Um, at the moment we are still a little bit in a halfway house of doing some stuff manually, so we are not as efficient as we could be and it makes it harder to draw some of those insights.

Um, I would just, uh, say that the, IM a database again was actually one of the things that came with mandatory registration. So we, we, we built it in 20 20, 20 21 as mandatory was coming in. And because in that year we had over 2000 applications, which was quite a high volume for us.

Um, you know, our focus was on get it built, let let, because that's when we, you're also moving to online, uh, applications. So that was our focus and now we, we've got a bit of clear air to go back, okay, what are the other things we need from it? So one of the areas, for example, is better understanding the flow of complaints and the trends and complaints.

So right now we can do manual stuff on that, it's fine, but actually to really dig in, we want to have the heavy lifting done by our IT infrastructure. Um, I can also have a provider assurance that we also do regular, um, cybersecurity audits. So we are pretty comfortable with our system.

We are in the cloud, but we've got good protections in place. Um, and again, I think it's one of the things we are small means we can be nimble and when we went into COVID, we, we got, uh, you know, people home and online probably within three or four days.

So unlike some of the big agencies which have, you know, multiple layers, that's the sort of thing that we can really navigate quite quickly. Um, but we wanna make sure that we are doing it with a really, um, keen eye on the risks also that come with some of the things that, that flexibility brings. And we don't wanna get into analyzing data and coming up with the wrong answers if we haven't got the right inputs.

I, I just want to add to that from the boss perspective. So there's an element of this, which is a kind of, we need to do the basics well, which is making sure the existing system functions well. But, um, uh, that if you like, the pressure from the board is looking ahead. So I know at the board meeting next week, there's a paper coming to us about the use of AI and how that might strengthen our processes.

Bit of a double-edged source for us because we can see how it may enhance what we do, but we are already experiencing the use of AI by applicants for social work registration, particularly from overseas. Um, and we need to, uh, we've kind of got a cautionary approach currently about how we manage that and our screening. Right. It's an area we need to consider carefully.

Okay, thank you. Um, Maureen, Um, thank you Mr. Chair. Um, so in terms of the legislation, you know, that's, that's stretched out the timeline now for registration. Have you got, um, a sense of how many are still in the workforce that are not registered? And what is the recruitment or the encouragement that you're able to give them?

Yeah, that's a very good question. I think, so again, I speak as a non practitioner, but, but probably, I mean, the advantage in a way of being a non practitioner is, is bringing that voice of the public or the consumer into the conversation. Um, social work doesn't have any restricted activities. So, so anyone can do things that a social worker does, but they can't call themselves a social worker.

And it means that drawing those boundaries around what is social work and what is not social work is, is quite difficult and often requires a conversation Attached to the select committee at the time. Yes, I can imagine, um, with the changes that came in earlier in the year that has given us, uh, the, the responsibility of investigating complaints where someone has been held out as a social worker but are not registered.

So this actually allows us at quite a sort of strategic level to engage in slightly different conversations. Um, you, you see in the presentation, you know, this regulatory pyramid of, of responsive regulation. And, and our starting point is always trying to educate, provide guidance, help people make the, the choice that that, that, you know, serves our purpose.

So in that complaint space for non-registered social workers, if there's the possibility for them to become registered, IE they hold a social work qualification, they're working in the social services or health space, people would have, you know, sort of on a, on a reasonable basis, think they're social work, a social worker, then we would be encouraging them to become registered.

And, and that's quite successful in terms of that wider population. There was a piece of work we did last year, um, on, it was as part of the, the, the work on the pay equity settlement for social workers where we looked at a category of people doing what was in effect social work according to the definitions within the pay equity process, but who were not registered.

So they were eligible for the pay equity settlement, but are not on our register. And they're around 1400 of those. Uh, we think there's probably a bigger pool because part of the conditions for that, uh, was that the, um, the role reported to a social worker. So there is, it's not to say there aren't some some public safety mechanisms around that.

Um, so, so when, when registration came in, we did quite a big information campaign, uh, and, and that was funded by government, which was fantastic. I think at the point now we're estimating, which is asked under 550 new registrations in the next year, there is still a pull if we can bring them over because, uh, it, it's this fundamental thing about a stronger profession together, but there is cost and compliance that comes with it.

So for people who can operate by doing social work, similar things, uh, that's still a valid pathway for them to do. And, and there will be other protections, whether it's at an employer level or, um, through, uh, through the, the child protection mechanisms.

I think in the longer term there's probably, um, a different conversation to have and, and in the review of the act, um, we are looking very much at just making what we do better. I think there will be things for the future, but we really need to make sure that we are doing the best we can now. And that includes trying to attract people in.

Uh, what happens to the group to the side is probably a future conversation. Uh, and again, from the board's perspective, we are looking at, I mean, I think, um, you're right, the section 13 pathways coming to an end. Yeah. Um, uh, and, um, what's becoming really clear from that?

So the section 13 pathway, as you probably know, um, is quite historic and was built really for a particular cohort of practitioners. And, and that cohort in a way is almost, is in many ways has aged out starting to age out. So what we are seeing in the section 13 applications, which Is an experience pathway, not just Experience, um, is that we are seeing people who want to convert their kind of current knowledge into social work being their social workers.

It might be teachers and so on, or people who want to step up. Um, so they've done a lot of, uh, support work, good, good practitioners, but actually want to get to the next stage of their career. Um, social, the section 13 process is not designed to manage that. So that's very much part of the conversation in the workforce strategy.

Um, and I believe MSD are doing work on future pathways, but that conversion one and that stepping up foot one is quite an important one. 'cause what we know about our workforce is we are aging, the average age is 45, um, and we know that the supply chain through academia is slowing down.

Mm-hmm. And there's a significant resource across New Zealand that we, you know, need to be tapped into. Mm-hmm. So there's an immediate answer for the, your section 13 question, other than we continue to see applications flow through and some very good ones that's that, um, that kind of meet that old kind of set of criteria. Um, but, but, but you know, it is on its way and it needs to be replaced and, and replaced in a way which meets the real needs that we are seeing in, in the people who do apply to be registered.

So supplementary, Laura, um, thank you Mr. Chair and thank you for, um, coming along today. And my question, I guess, kind of follows on from what you're talking about. So what would be the biggest benefit of then being registered and how do you plan on making that more attractive in order to draw over the ones that aren't? Because like you said, some of them got the paid equity, so they don't have to then deal with the bureaucracy of being registered.

So what is the goal to get them? So I think, I mean, it's, it, it's, it's, it's an extremely good question 'cause it actually speaks to the heart of both what we do. But occupational regulation in general, I guess I'd say we are, we are slightly different from a number of occupational regulators because we also have this workforce function.

Mm-hmm. And it means that we sort of integrated in thinking, you know, regulation through to workforce, uh, impacts that other regulators might not consider in the same way when we did the information campaign, it, it really was the message about stronger together.

And I think that's still valid. Part of the, part of the framing is so, so people are involved in this profession because they wanna make a difference. You know, they may have lived experience, um, that they wanna turn into something positive. Um, so, so there's lots of opportunity there.

It's helping them understand that part of the flip side is being able to demonstrate that accountability to the communities they work with. So for some, they will already feel that they have those accountability mechanisms, uh, to, to whanau to the community. We, we need to be positioning that there's another layer that with, you know, if you're an employer, you have, um, a way of managing some of the risk because you've got some external assurance of the people you're employing as a practitioner at an individual level.

It's not so much about you as the individual, it's building that system trust. So there's a, there's a piece of work that we are doing. And I guess really what I'm trying to say there, there is no silver bullet. It's lots of pieces joined up and there's a piece of work that we did a couple of years ago that we are refreshing as part of our doing the review of the act, which is around public trust in the profession.

Because in the end, that's, that's, you know, sort of why, why we're here. And, and there's some challenges, I think, for the profession and the wider community about what their aspirations as a profession are. And if part of that is that they're working in a profession that is trusted and valued, then one of the ways they demonstrate that is participating in the, in, in the regulatory framework, because that's part of what enhances professionalism.

But that's quite a, that's quite a long journey to get someone through when actually they just wanna be delivering on the ground to this person now. So, so I think, um, you know, that idea of engagement and using the opportunity of this new function to be reaching out to those, um, people who might be doing social work who are not registered, allows us to start to have some of those wider conversations.

And it's a shift for both the individual practitioner but also the employer. One of the things that social work is very fortunate in, in my view, is that a very high proportion of employers support their practitioners through praying, paying their annual practicing certificate. Um, but we need also employers to be stepping alongside us because if we try to like recruit, you know, maybe, you know, 200 extra social workers, if employers aren't in a position to back them through CPD through supervision, through praying their practicing certificate,

we'd set up a situation where actually we could be setting people up to not be successful as we'd want to. So that's what I mean, it's a multi, it's a multi-pronged approach. And, and as Mike said, you know, social work has been mandatory since 2021. You think of professions like nursing or, or, or law, um, or accountancy.

They've been professions for many, many, many, many, many years. So we are at the start of the journey and, and when I came into the role, I thought it will take at least five years. I actually now think it will probably take at least 10. Right? We've got a good base, but actually this is a culture shift for the sector and for the public in terms of them understanding what they can expect.

So it's, it's two sides of the coin. So I know that's a very roundabout answer, but it just reflects that it's, I I could just say we're gonna do an information campaign, but, but it needs to be more than that. I, I think, if I've heard your question correctly, um, I think anecdotally, I don't think we would think there are too many people who are social workers who are not registered.

'cause there's always been the power, but vest in somebody else to investigate people who might be representing themselves as social workers, but not be registered. I think those numbers are very small. That function has now come to us, and that may well provide em with some insights. So I, it's, so I think so. So when we're talking about other social workers who are, who are not registered, I think the answer is they may be very small because people are well aware, very responsible employers are what we're, I think

what Sarah's talking about is that wide professional base people that are doing the work. Yeah. So there are people who might be doing work that is similar but actually couldn't be registered 'cause they wouldn't meet the very clear criteria. Can I, um, Ask a supplement supplementary will Look, I, I, um, just my background as I had many, many social workers work for me in my previous roles. Some of my best social workers were not qualified. Mm-hmm.

So I'm just, uh, you know, and you know, some of my social work workers, ex gang members, um, they come off the streets. They just want to give, they want to redeem themselves. One of the big complaints they had was, was this, is that, that you've made it too hard for some of them to continue.

And so I'm worried about, you know, that, that you, you're balancing this public trust in the profession with the reality, which is that some of our best now are missing out. Now. That's what they were saying to me. Uh, they've been saying to me for years now, I've, obviously, I'm not in that role, but I'm still connected in that role anyway as a chairman of a a.

So, you know, I still see my old social workers and a number of them have come out, fallen out of the profession. So, um, I'm, I'm worried about where we're at. Uh, you think you've, you know, we're just too tough. Now in terms of this, are you worrying too much about the public, uh, the public, uh, you know, where the public essence in terms of the reality on the street?

Um, well, I suppose I wouldn't disagree with that. I guess the reality is that if the pathway for those people, the section 13 pathway is coming to an end. And, and so the challenge is about what do you put in place in, in, in place of that to meet the kind of need you've described.

Mm-hmm. I think that, and I don't think there's any, there's no denial, I think of what you've seen. I think that is right. Um, you know, the current criteria other than section 13 is that you hold a, um, professional qualification for really clear, when we look at, um, uh, professional qualifications of academic route, for many people, that is challenging.

Yes. Um, both in terms of entry, in terms of cost, the length of the degree, because I think we're gonna miss out on something, aren't we? You know, you know, in terms of the Well, there's, well, there's a, well, if you've got an aging workforce and it's, and we're losing social workers, there's a workforce out there, we need to, um, no, I'm talking about that. I'm talking about what these people bring to the table. Yes.

Yes. I think I'm saying the same thing. I think we're missing out. You're right. I think, I think if I could just add too, that we also hear concerns about people who have, for various reasons, things in their past that they may perceive as being a barrier. Yes. What I would say is, is there is, that's probably more at the perception level rather than the reality because, um, because we on a regular basis have applications before the board where individuals have, have, uh, have lists of con convictions.

The question for the board at that point is balancing this person has a, has a past. And one of the things they'll often ask themselves is, have they taken, undertaken their qualification after, after those, those experiences. So the, the education has been part of their redemption redemptive journey.

And by and large, the board, if it feels that the, the safety can be managed, we'll register them. So, so convictions isn't so much of a problem. It's people thinking it might be a problem and, and maybe hesitating to, to reach out. Um, but we know that there's a good portion of of those who do.

And I think, I think we've got quite a high proportion of our, um, of those who apply, who we can successfully navigate through might be with some conditions. You know, it might be get supervision, it might be, um, a report back to us in progress in six months. But often those situations can be managed.

It's really people having the confidence to come to us in the first place and ask the question, is this gonna be a barrier? Um, thank you. Uh, couple, one, Mike to you and then to Sarah, Mike. So your new board of, you've got some positions to fill. Understand. So how, um, do you have any, um, Maori competency on your board?

Do you have Maori individuals competent and able to bring that To you? Yes, we, we do. Um, and our, uh, current, um, um, potential gap in the board is around Pacifica. Mm-hmm. Um, and, and so, um, uh, the current recruitment round is looking at that as a need. Um, thank you. I just wanted to, the Other bit, I'd say we're also supported by, uh, Kahui, Right. Maori Advisor group, um, with whom, yes.

Um, I, as the board chair have a very direct relationship. Um, What's the numbers, what's the numbers of Maori on your book? There are one Maori on the Bar and six non-Maori. Six non-Maori. Okay. Okay. That needs to change. Just offering that advice. Um, Sarah, so I guess it's a similar thing. I'm keen to know on the ground mm-hmm.

Um, the, the competency 'cause so, uh, credentialed social worker, but not registered mm-hmm. Decided just got a bit too, um, in my way Yeah. To be frank. Yeah. And that is because it didn't match my level of competency mm-hmm. And ability to do the work. Mm-hmm. And it's a little bit like what Willie was saying.

I think there's a, there's a perception that it should, and, you know, it might technically, but from a cultural mm-hmm. Uh, imperative, that's what failed then. And I am concerned with all the best intention that it still mm-hmm. Doesn't deliver. Mm-hmm. Um, and well, it doesn't, does I, we've Got two more people course one ask questions as Well of my apologies. Yeah.

Just Wanna get some confidence from You. Yeah. So one of the challenges that the outgoing chair presented, uh, was, uh, that the organization truly become responsive. Um, and I think we have made good progress. We have, um, we have, I think it's about 8% of staff identify as Maori.

Uh, but more importantly, we have taken, um, time to really embed out Maori development framework because actually our ability to engage doesn't rest on our Maori staff. It rests on all of us. So our Maori staff are supported, but actually we need to step up.

Uh, and I think I, I feel quite confident, like, I'm not saying we have everything perfect, and I'm not saying we're at the end of the journey, but our staff are really committed to, to working with Maori, to working with diverse communities, uh, in a way that, uh, supports the person to engage with us.

Well, even if say in the complaint space, it's something that there are some difficult conversations. Our ability to actually work with people is really important. And, and one of the things that, um, uh, that's side aside, and it's maybe a little, it might feel a bit light, but we've just moved into a new office this week and around the office, uh, are, um, what looked like our koku panels of, um, the representation of our various work areas.

And staff are immensely proud of them because they understand the significance to all, to all communities. But in particular, it's the challenge to ourselves. And we see that on our register around 25%, identify as Maori for a regulated profession that's pretty high. That, that challenges us to make sure we continue to deliver in a way that works. Thank you, sir.

I'm gonna, um, One minute. So I have to ask some very concise questions and answers. Follow Just a quick one, which has already been touched a few times mm-hmm. About complaints. Mm-hmm. Um, what is the, do you have a top three, um, complaints issue? And does this inform the work of mm-hmm. Mm-hmm.

Mm-hmm. Yes. That's my very short answer. Yes. Um, the top three would be, um, would be, well, the complaints that come in the door would actually be things that don't necessarily meet the threshold because they are related to some sort of vexatious or element of that, of that non-choice relationship.

Uh, and then I'd say, uh, boundaries, management of boundaries and, uh, and probably, um, co low, low levels of conduct or communication. So currently we have nine investigations at a complaints level, like at a next elevated level, PCC level.

So the volume of complaints is high, but the seriousness of them is quite low. And last question, Helen. Thank you. Um, first all I was gonna say, I realize it's an incredibly difficult job being a social worker and, um, trying to, um, give that shape and, um, appropriate boundaries must be extremely challenging.

So thank you for everything that you're doing. Um, in your, um, your model, your triangle here. Mm-hmm. You've got, um, that sort of the base of your triangle being the core competency standards and, um, the building the social worker capability. Um, I very much appreciate the lived experiences coming into this picture.

Can you just tell us in a really practical way, what are the most important things that we can do and we can fund, uh, in that area of building safe capability for people that may be coming from a background where they haven't had that kind of discipline? A big question I have to ask. Short answer A good question. Uh, maybe I'll, maybe I'll, I'll, I'll I'll say where our current thinking is, because that's a future possibility.
